summary: Gruber argues that the iPhone X represents an unprecedented attempt to completely rethink a massively successful platform without forcing a painful, disruptive transition on users. He draws parallels to the classic Mac OS to Mac OS X transition, which took nearly a decade and broke existing software. By contrast, Apple is evolving iOS one device at a time — the iPhone X introduces fundamentally different interaction paradigms (Face ID, gesture navigation, no home button) while the iPhone 8 preserves the familiar experience. After two months of use, Gruber concludes the iPhone X succeeds as a conceptual modernization that feels like the natural evolution of the platform rather than change for its own sake.
key_points: The iPhone X effectively creates two distinct versions of iOS 11 — one gesture-based for iPhone X and one home-button-based for everything else — which is unprecedented in Apple history
Face ID's killer advantage over Touch ID is that it removes friction when it works (which is most of the time), making the phone feel unlocked rather than just easier to unlock
Apple took a platform with two primary interaction methods (touchscreen and home button), removed one, and created a better, more integrated experience
The existence of iPhone 8 as a familiar alternative allows Apple to make radical changes on iPhone X without forcing anyone to adapt, enabling a gradual platform-wide transition
The iPhone X's interaction changes are faithful to the spirit of the original iPhone — Face ID effectively restores the frictionless 'slide to unlock' experience Jobs originally demoed
money_quotes: A platform is only 'finished' when it is abandoned.
Eventually, inevitably, incremental improvements paint a platform into a corner. Something has to give.
Apple took a platform with two primary means of interacting with the apps — a touchscreen and a home button — removed one of them, and created a better, more integrated, more organic experience.
Touch ID feels like a better way to unlock your device. Face ID feels like your device isn't even locked.
The familiarity of iPhone 8 allows iPhone X to reinvent anything, to break the platform's foundational conventions.
Ossification is a risk with a platform as popular and successful as the iPhone — fear of making unpopular changes can lead a platform vendor to make no significant changes.
If you're going to have a bump, have a fucking bump.
I truly consider the iPhone home button icon the single best icon ever. It perfectly represented anything and everything apps could be — it was iconic in every sense of the word.
key_insight: The iPhone X is Apple's unprecedented attempt to fundamentally reinvent its most successful platform one device at a time, avoiding the traumatic transition that plagued the classic Mac OS to OS X shift.
